titleStmt.title: Introduction to the Site of Mount Long

div.head: 龍山

figure.head: Mount Long, eastern part of the cliff; photograph taken in 2008
龍山東面崖壁。2008年拍攝

div.p: 對此小型刻經址的調查始於2008年。此處只有三個字可辨識；此三字出自《文殊師利所説般若波羅蜜經》九十八字節文。原始的刊刻時間一般認爲是6世紀。少數幾個造像龕的年代暫定爲隋或唐朝。另有983紀年的刻字。

figure.head: Mount Long, comprehensive view of cliff with niches and location of LS 1; photograph taken in 2008
龍山，包括佛龕的崖壁全景及龍山1號的位置。2008年拍攝

div.head: 空間
div.p: 龍山是山東省最南端的刻經址，位於滕州市柴胡店鎮南辛村以東。柴胡店鎮東部爲丘陵地，有46座小山，以大南山最高，海拔287米。
銘文刊刻於龍山南面的一座山的南坡上。新近發表的研究文獻稱該山爲羅漢山。賴非第150頁。《山東佛教刻經全集》第1册，第52頁。其地理位置是東經117.279590度，北緯34.903425度，相當於UTM坐標系統第50S帶，東行525543，北行3862369。

對此經址的調查始於2008年9月17日。有一佛教遺迹緊鄰高約8米，東西寬約28米的陡峭崖壁，該遺址原來可能是進行禮拜儀式之所或寺院。附近有磚牆殘垣。崖壁東側上半部有成排的小龕，每一小龕各有一尊佛像及脅侍菩薩（）。風格上似爲隋唐之作。崖壁西側平視處，有一爲粗糙的墨水題字所環繞的小造像龕，引人注意（）。該龕正下方可辨識出造像題記片段。供養人的名字顯著，他們被稱爲“施主”，可能是漢譯“陀那施主”的簡稱。此外，賴非轉録了一個確切的日期，即太平興國八年九月十八日（983年10月26日）。賴非第32頁。賴非第150頁。 《山東佛教刻經全集》第1册，第52頁。在據稱爲刻經區的左側及沿其下緣有綫雕，這些綫雕有些僅是花紋及裝飾紋，但有些可能是人物圖像的殘紋（）。
在崖壁中央部分、毗鄰崖壁東側佛龕，距今地面高度約三米處發現了一段佛經節文的其中三個字。打製拓片時，需要一個梯子或由附近的岩石攀爬而上，纔能觸及刻字（）。

div.head: 時間
div.p: 除了崖壁西側的983年祈願文外，此刻經址不見其他日期。不過，由於佛經節文僅存的三個刻字緊鄰崖壁東側可能是隋或唐代的佛像，且其書法與其他山東地區北齊刻銘接近，再加上這三個字可辨識出爲出自在山東已刊刻十餘次的《文殊師利所説般若波羅蜜經》九十八字節文，關於九十八字節文刊刻的列表，見。龍山的佛經殘文因此符合北齊和北周時山東石刻經的歷史背景。
經文的下方，賴非釋讀出寺名“隱山寺”。雖然《柴胡店鎮誌》描述了這個地區少數幾座寺院的情况，但並未提及一名爲隱山寺的寺院。《柴胡店鎮誌》第386—388頁。
此外，拓自龍山1號下方的石頭的拓片，即賴非所發現的寺院名，明顯顯示出“隱”字並未與其他兩字“山”及“寺”字排列成一行（），因此，這三個字可能根本不是一個寺院名。

div.head: 刻銘
div.p: 賴非提供了《文殊師利所説般若波羅蜜經》九十八字節文的復原圖，這篇復原圖共16行，除了第1行多了兩個字之外，每行6字。殘存的三字分别位於第15行第5字、第15行第6字及第16行第6字。賴非第33頁。賴非第151頁。《山東佛教刻經全集》第1册，第52頁。這樣的安排是有道理的，因爲刻於山東的九十八字節文文本皆相同，没有不同的讀法。不過，此處可能將“文殊師利”之名簡化爲“文殊”，以達到16行每行6字的規整設計（見下文龍山1號的復原文字設計）。雖然在經文殘存的三個字上面有一道大裂縫，但此三字上方和右邊的其他崖壁表面可能恰可以爲這樣的佈局提供足夠的空間（）。

div.head: Mount Long
div.p: This small site was first surveyed in 2008. Only three characters could be identified; they are recognized as belonging to the 98 character passage from the Sutra on the Great Perfection of Wisdom Spoken by Mañjuśrī (Wenshushili suo shuo mohe bore boluomi jing 文殊師利所説摩訶般若波羅蜜經). The original engraving is thought to date from the sixth century. A few image niches are provisionally dated to the Sui or Tang dynasties, and there is an engraved date of 983.

div.head: Space
div.p: Mount Long 龍山 is the southernmost inscription site in Shandong Province. It is situated to the east of Nanxin Village 南辛村 in Chaihudian Town 柴胡店鎮, Tengzhou City 滕州市. The eastern part of the area of Chaihudian Town is covered with low hills. Of these 46 peaks the highest, the Great Southern Mountain 大南山, reaches up to 287 m above sea level.

The inscription is located on the southern slope of the mountain to the south of Mount Long 龍山. In the most recent publications, this mountain is called Mount Luohan 羅漢山. 150; 1: 52. The geographic position is 117.279590° east and 34.903425° north WGS84. This equals 525543 E (easting) and 3862369 N (northing) on the meridian 50S UTM.

A survey was conducted on September 17, 2008. The ruins of a Buddhist site of worship, or even a monastery, were found next to a steep cliff, which is about 8 m high, and stretches about 28 m from west to east. Close by are the remains of brick walls. On the upper part of the eastern cliff section, rows of small niches are located, each with a Buddha figure and accompanying bodhisattvas (fig. ). Stylistically, they seem to be from the Sui or Tang dynasties. On the western cliff section, a small image niche at eye level, surrounded by coarse ink inscriptions, catches the attention of the visitor (fig. ). Directly below, fragments of a votive inscription can be deciphered. Conspicuous are names of donors, who are called “alms givers” (shizhu 施主), probably a short form of the Chinese transliteration of dānapati (tuonashizhu 陀那施主). Lai Fei also transcribes an exact date, the eighteenth day of the ninth month of the eighth year of the taiping xingguo 太平興國 era (October 26, 983). 32; 150; 1: 52. To the left of the supposed field of script, and along its lower border, are carved lines, some merely floral and decorative, but some may be fragments of figures (fig. ).

On the central cliff section, adjacent to the niches in the eastern section, three characters of a sutra passage were discovered at a height of about 3 m above today’s ground level. In order to take a rubbing, it is necessary to use a ladder or to stand on a protruding rock to reach up to the characters (fig. ).

figure.head: Mount Long, western cliff section with Buddha niches and votive inscription dated to 983; photograph taken in 2008
龍山西邊斷崖剖面與佛龕及983年造像題記。2008年拍攝

div.head: Time
div.p: Apart from the votive inscription on the western cliff section of 983, no other date is available on the site. However, the three remaining characters of the sutra passage are located close to the eastern cliff section with earlier Buddha figures probably of the Sui or Tang dynasties. The calligraphy resembles that of other Shandong carvings from the Northern Qi dynasty. Moreover, the characters can be recognized as belonging to the often seen 98 character passage taken from the Sutra on the Great Perfection of Wisdom Spoken by Mañjuśrī , which was carved about a dozen times in Shandong.For a list see . The fragmented sutra text thus fits well with the Shandong stone sutras carved under the Northern Qi and Northern Zhou dynasties.

Below the sutra text, Lai Fei has deciphered the name of a monastery, reading Yinshan Monastery 隱山寺. Although the Gazetteer of Chaihudian Town (Chaihudian Zhen zhi 柴胡鎮誌) describes the conditions of a few monasteries of this area, it does not mention a monastery with such a name. 386–388. In addition, the rubbing taken from the rock section below LS 1, where Lai Fei found the monastery name, clearly shows that the character yin 隱 is not aligned with the two other characters, shan 山 and si 寺 (fig. ), and thus may not be a monastery name at all.
There is also no available information on related persons.

figure.head: Mount Long, detail of western cliff section with fragments of votive inscription and names of donors; photograph taken in 2008
龍山西邊崖壁造像題記和供養人名，局部。2008年拍攝

div.head: Inscription
div.p: Lai Fei has offered a reconstruction of the 98 character passage from the Sutra on the Great Perfection of Wisdom Spoken by Mañjuśrī , in which the surviving three characters take the positions 15/5, 15/6 and 16/6 in an arrangement of this text in 16 columns with six characters in each column, with the exception of the first column, which has two more characters. 33; 151; 1: 52. Such an arrangement is reasonable, because the texts of the passage carved in Shandong are all identical, without any variant readings. However, it is possible that the name of Mañjuśrī, Wenshushili 文殊師利, may have been shortened to Wenshu 文殊 in this case, to obtain a regular layout of 16 x 6 characters (see the reconstructed layout text of LS 1 below). There is a large crevice above the three surviving characters of the sutra text, but the remaining space on the cliff surface above and to the right of them may have offered just enough space for such a layout (fig. ).
